1:
The witnesses agree on the plain event and read it as the first stroke in God's long controversy with Jeroboam. The grammatical-historical commentators reconstruct the whole manoeuvre from the single verse. Keil and Delitzsch note that when his son Abijah fell sick, Jeroboam bade his wife disguise herself — the verb means 'to alter one's self' — and go to Shiloh to Ahijah, the very prophet who had once told him he should be king over this people (1 Kings 11:29), hoping that the man whose earlier word had come true would now foretell the boy's fate. They add the shrewd calculation behind it: because Jeroboam had contemptuously neglected Ahijah's admonition, he did not otherwise promise himself a favourable answer, and so resorted to a disguise. Ellicott lingers on the name: Abijah means 'whose father is Jehovah,' and he flags the curious coincidence that Jeroboam and Rehoboam each named a son so — perhaps more than coincidence, if the two births fell about the same time, while Jeroboam still stood in Solomon's favour.

The Reformed Gill reads the sickness as chastisement: the child was 'smitten of God with some disease, as a punishment of Jeroboam's sin,' though he honestly grants that no one can say how long after the idolatry of the previous chapter this affliction fell.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws out the pastoral weight the others leave implicit. Here, he says, God proceeds in his controversy with a sinner, 'for when God judges he will overcome, and sinners shall either bend or break before him.' The blow lands on the eldest and likeliest son, heir to the crown; dignity, youth, and even the boy's own piety could not exempt him from dangerous sickness, and Henry turns it on every reader — let none be secure of the continuance of their health, but improve it while it lasts. Above all he presses the summons in the timing: at the very moment Jeroboam profaned the priesthood (1 Kings 13:33), his child sickened; and when sickness enters our families we should inquire 'whether there be not some particular sin harboured in our houses' which the affliction is sent to convince us of and reclaim us from.

The Wesleyan Clarke frames the stroke as of a piece with the mercy that runs through the chapter. The sickness 'was but a prelude to the miseries which fell on the house of Jeroboam,' yet also 'another merciful warning, intended to turn him from his idolatry and wickedness.' Henry sharpens a last irony: Jeroboam, who had slighted the prophet in prosperity, now in distress sends to him — 'Lord, in trouble have those visited thee who before slighted thee' — yet those who by sin disqualify themselves for comfort, and still expect their ministers to speak them peace, 'greatly wrong both themselves and their ministers.' The voices do not divide; in their hands the sick child of a king becomes at once the opening of a judgment and a last knock at a hard door.
2:
The witnesses fasten on the strange errand and its deeper irony: the king who had forsaken the God of Israel now sends secretly to that God's prophet. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the history. Keil and Delitzsch explain why Jeroboam turned precisely to Ahijah — it was he who had 'spoken concerning him to be king' (1 Kings 11:31), and since that favourable prophecy had come to pass, he 'hoped that he might also obtain from Ahijah a divine revelation concerning the result of his son's illness.' The disguise, they add, was meant to deceive the old prophet, since the king, having contemptuously neglected Ahijah's earlier admonition (1 Kings 11:38), did not otherwise promise himself a kindly answer.

Ellicott turns to the place. Shiloh, once the home of the ark and of Eli, is barely named in Scripture after the great Philistine defeat and the destruction that seems to have overtaken it (Jeremiah 7:12); yet the blind old prophet still remained there, exercising his office for the good of Israel while standing aloof from, and denouncing, the new idolatry of Bethel. That idolatry is always styled pre-eminently 'the sin of Jeroboam, who made Israel to sin.' Ellicott draws the poignant consequence: though the royal house is condemned for it, the people are still God's chosen, and to them — bereft now of temple and Davidic king — the prophetic ministry was of the highest importance, so that the northern kingdom's chief remaining witness for God was the very word Jeroboam now sought to manipulate rather than obey. Accordingly the queen is bidden approach the prophet disguised as a daughter of the people.

The Reformed Gill adds only that the woman's own name is nowhere recorded, a silence the narrative keeps deliberately. The witnesses do not contend here; together they expose the divided heart of the apostate. Jeroboam still believes Ahijah can read the future, and in his distress he passes by the calves he set up and seeks the one man in Israel who speaks for the living God — yet he comes cloaked, wanting the prophet's knowledge without the prophet's rebuke, the oracle of the future without the summons of the present. The very disguise that hopes to hide the queen becomes the measure of a king who will use God's servant but not obey God's word.
3:
The little inventory of gifts — ten loaves, cracknels, and a cruse of honey — draws the witnesses into the custom of approaching a prophet, and into the danger lurking within it. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the background. Ellicott notes that the offering was 'designedly simple and rustic,' fitting the disguise, and accords with the standing practice of coming to the seer with some present, however trifling (1 Samuel 9:7); yet he warns that an act meant simply as homage 'would easily degenerate into the treatment of the prophetic function as a mere matter of merchandise' (1 Kings 13:7). Clarke fills in the detail of the food: the cracknels are 'spotted, or perforated bread,' thin cakes pierced with many holes, 'the same as is called Jews' bread to the present day,' and he confirms that no one consulted a prophet without bringing something in his hand.

The Reformed Gill reads the present through the lens of the disguise. The gifts are 'a plain present, and of such things as the country afforded,' so that the queen might be taken for a plain countrywoman and not for the personage she was; the ten loaves were light enough for a woman to carry, most likely of wheat, and the cracknels, marked and pricked for ornament, were perhaps for the prophet's children. Gill adds the philological touch that the cracknels take their Hebrew name from the points or pricks made in them for the sake of ornament, likening them to the marked cakes the ancients called by a word meaning 'written,' as though scored and pierced. Everything about the offering is calculated to conceal.

The witnesses do not divide, but their combined reading is quietly telling. The very ordinariness of the basket that was meant to hide a queen exposes the calculation of the household that packed it: Jeroboam will spend on the errand only what a peasant might, lest a lavish gift betray the sender. And the custom itself, honourable in origin, sits under Ellicott's caution — that homage to God's messenger can curdle into a bribe, treating the prophetic function as a thing that money buys. The queen carries bread and honey to a man who cannot be bought; the present that hopes to purchase a comfortable oracle will be met, in the next verses, only with the plain and heavy word of the Lord, which no cruse of honey can sweeten.
4:
The witnesses gather on a single irony: the prophet cannot see the woman, yet he will see straight through her. The grammatical-historical commentators handle the physical fact with care. Keil and Delitzsch explain that Ahijah 'could no longer see, because his eyes were blinded with age' — the same idiom used of Eli (1 Samuel 4:15) — and identify the condition as amaurosis, the black cataract; it was therefore all the less possible for him to recognise the visitor in any natural way. Ellicott presses the same medical note: the word rendered 'set' is elsewhere translated 'dim,' the metaphor drawn from 'the solid opaque look of the iris' when clouded by cataract or some similar disease.

The Reformed Gill keeps the human scene in view. The queen 'disguised herself, and took this long journey, and found the prophet's house,' moved partly by obedience to her husband and partly by a mother's affection for her child; but Ahijah 'could not see her or anybody else that came into the room to him.' Gill lets the plain sentence stand, and its force is the greater for its plainness.

The convergence of the witnesses lands on a lesson the verse itself sets up. Every human advantage in this meeting belongs to the concealment: a disguise, a rustic present, a long anonymous road, and now a blind old man who cannot so much as make out the face before him. The pathos of the scene is deliberate — a queen who has schooled herself to deceive stands before a man who literally cannot see the deception, so that every natural advantage lies with the lie. Yet the witnesses are agreed that the advantage is illusory. Keil and Delitzsch note the one fact that overturns them all: before ever she arrived, the Lord had told his servant she was coming and what he was to say. The blindness that should have made deception easy only sharpens the marvel — the prophet's failing eyes see nothing, and yet, by revelation, he will name the queen at the door. The witnesses thus prepare the reader for the collision of a household's cleverness with the God to whom darkness and light are both alike, so that the elaborate stratagem is undone before the first word is spoken.
5:
The verse turns the tables in advance, and the witnesses concentrate on the forewarning that makes the disguise useless. The grammatical-historical commentators trace the mechanism. Keil and Delitzsch observe that before the woman's arrival the Lord not only revealed her coming and her errand but told the prophet exactly what to say, so that the blind man is armed against her pretence: 'thus and thus shalt thou say unto her.' The briefing is complete — not only that she is coming, and why, but the very words he must speak — so that the prophet is furnished beforehand against every turn of her disguise. The address that follows springs not from Ahijah's eyes but from God's word.

The Reformed Gill notes the manner of the revelation — 'either in a dream, or by an impulse upon his mind, before Jeroboam's wife came in' — leaving the how open while insisting on the fact that God spoke first. The prophet's knowledge is borrowed, and borrowed from the only source that cannot be deceived.

The Wesleyan Clarke seizes the irony that the whole disguise betrays. Why should the queen 'feign herself to be another woman'? Because, he answers, 'it would have been discreditable to Jeroboam's calves if it had been known that he had consulted a prophet of Jehovah.' The concealment is thus a confession: in his extremity the king abandons the gods he had made and turns in secret to the God he had abandoned, and he dares not let it be seen. The witnesses do not contend; they let the two truths stand side by side. On the one hand the household's caution is shrewd, covering the queen from her own court, from the countryside, and from the prophet himself; on the other hand it is doomed, for the God who is not mocked has already whispered the whole plot into a blind man's ear. Clarke's insight gives the disguise its full irony, since the concealment by which Jeroboam hoped to protect the honour of his calves is itself a silent admission that, in the hour of need, the calves are useless and only the God of Israel can tell him what shall be. So the stage is set for the queen to hear her own name spoken by a man who never saw her come.
6:
The moment of recognition draws the witnesses to the collision of a disguise with the God who sees, and the Puritan Matthew Henry carries the weight of it. As the queen steps through the door, Ahijah, forewarned, calls her by her very name: 'Come in, thou wife of Jeroboam; why feignest thou thyself to be another? for I am sent to thee with heavy tidings.' Henry marks that the prophet has no regard to three things men usually defer to. Not to her rank — she was a queen, 'but what was that to him, who had a message to deliver to her immediately from God, before whom all the children of men stand upon the same level?' Not to her present, for though it was fair to accept a token of respect, he owed her no finer language than her message required. And not to her careful concealment; the prophet 'was no courtier, nor gave flattering titles,' but plain dealing is best, and she shall know at the first word what she has to trust to.

The grammatical-historical commentators anchor the scene in its detail. Keil and Delitzsch fix the exact instant — 'when Ahijah heard the sound of her feet, as she came in at the door' — and note that the whole disclosure hangs on the sound of footsteps, not on sight; the blind prophet knows her before she can speak. The Reformed Gill lets the bare narrative stand, that it was the noise of her feet at the threshold that prompted the greeting.

From this Henry draws the searching application in which the witnesses concur. 'Those who think by their disguises to hide themselves from God will be wretchedly confounded' in the day of discovery. Sinners now go about 'in the garb of saints, and are taken to be such,' but they will blush and tremble when stripped of their false colours and called by their own name. Be not deceived, he warns, God is not mocked (Galatians 6:7); he 'will judge men according to what they are, not according to what they seem,' and tidings of a portion with the hypocrites 'will be heavy tidings.' The queen came in as a stranger and is met as the wife of Jeroboam — a disguise that fooled her own court and the countryside undone by a single word from a man sent by God, a small parable of the great unmasking that awaits every counterfeit.
7:
With the queen unmasked, the prophet turns from her to her husband, and the witnesses read the oracle as a divine indictment built on the memory of grace despised. The grammatical-historical commentators lay out its structure. Keil and Delitzsch trace the logic: 'because I exalted thee from among the people, and made thee prince over my people Israel' (1 Kings 11:31), yet thou hast not been as my servant David, but 'hast done worse than all who were before thee,' making other gods and casting me behind thy back — an expression, they note, found only here and in Ezekiel 23:35, denoting the most scornful contempt of God, the exact opposite of keeping him before the eyes and in the heart. The oracle piles the counts of the charge — exalted from the people, made prince over Israel, worse than all before him, making molten gods, casting the Lord behind his back — so that the sentence, when it comes, falls upon a case already proven.

The Puritan Matthew Henry expounds the charge as a study in ingratitude. God names himself 'the Lord God of Israel,' for though Israel had forsaken him he had not cast them off; and he upbraids Jeroboam with the great favour he had bestowed — raising him from among the common people to rule God's chosen, and taking the kingdom from the house of David to give it him. Henry presses the principle: 'whether we keep an account of God's mercies to us or no, he does, and will set even them in order before us, if we be ungrateful, to our greater confusion,' though to the thankful he 'gives and upbraids not.' The messenger, he adds, could be no one but the king's own wife; a servant would have smothered such a message, but she cannot be suspected of ill-will to him.

The Reformed Gill simply identifies the addressee — 'Go tell Jeroboam,' that is, thy husband — and lets the sentence run on into the doom. The witnesses do not divide; they hear in the opening 'forasmuch as' the whole moral logic of the judgment. Jeroboam's crown was a gift, held on the plain condition of obedience that Ahijah himself had once announced (1 Kings 11:38); and it is precisely the enormity of the mercy that measures the enormity of the apostasy. The God who gave the kingdom now recounts the giving, not to boast, but to convict, so that when the sentence falls the sinner may have nothing to plead.
8:
The prophet sets David before Jeroboam as the standard he has spurned, and the witnesses weigh the contrast between the king who was given a kingdom and the king who kept faith. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the terms of comparison. Keil and Delitzsch read the sentence as one long antithesis: God 'rent the kingdom away from the house of David and gave it thee,' and yet 'thou hast not been as my servant David, who kept my commandments, and who followed me with all his heart, to do that only which was right in mine eyes.' They underline that the standard is not outward conformity but the heart — David 'followed me with all his heart' — so that Jeroboam's fault is measured against a wholeness of devotion, not a flawless record; the measure of him is not his own worst rivals but the best of the kings before him.

The Wesleyan Clarke supplies a careful theological nuance on the rending. God 'rent the kingdom away from the house of David,' Clarke observes, means that he 'permitted it to be rent, because of the folly and insolence of Rehoboam' — the division was a judgment on Solomon's house, not an arbitrary act, and Jeroboam was the instrument, not the author, of it. Even the gift he received was thus a chapter of God's righteous dealing, not a licence for his idolatry, and that very fact deepens his guilt: the throne he holds is a standing testimony to God's providence, which he has answered with idolatry.

The Reformed Gill quantifies the gift — 'even ten parts out of twelve' — so that the reader feels the disproportion between what Jeroboam received and what he returned. The witnesses do not contend; together they make David the silent rebuke that runs through the oracle. David was no sinless man, yet 'he followed God with all his heart,' and his faithful adherence to the worship of the true God won him the character here held up as an example to all his successors. Jeroboam had every advantage David had, and one David never had — a kingdom handed to him ready-made — and he used it to invent a rival religion. The comparison is not flattery of a dead king but the plumb-line by which the living one is found crooked, and by which his house is about to be pulled down.
9:
Here the oracle reaches its sharpest charge — 'thou hast done evil above all that were before thee' — and the witnesses take up the difficult question whether the words can be literally true. The Reformed Gill meets it head on by ranging Jeroboam against his predecessors: he has done worse 'not only above David, but above Saul, who never gave into idolatry, yea, even above Solomon, who, though he connived at idolatry, yet did not attempt to draw his people into it.' The distinction is decisive for Gill — Jeroboam is the first to make idolatry the settled religion of the state.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott feels the full weight of the language and wrestles with it. It is strong, he admits, 'in the face of the many instances of the worship of false gods in the days of the Judges, and the recent apostasy of Solomon.' His answer runs on two lines. First, the guilt was enhanced by 'the presumptuous contempt of the special promise of God, given on the sole condition of obedience' — which is why Jeroboam alone is said to have cast God himself behind his back. Second, and chiefly, the sin is measured by its unprecedented effect: 'other idolatries came and passed away; this continued, and at all times made Israel to sin.' Ellicott adds that the molten images cannot be excused as mere emblems (1 Kings 12:28); Jeroboam would have justified the calves as tokens of the true God who brought Israel out of Egypt, but Ahijah rejects the plea outright, holding these forbidden images to be really 'other gods,' as all experience shows such emblems eventually become; and from 1 Kings 14:15 it appears that the foul worship of the Asherah had already attached itself to the calves.

The witnesses do not so much divide as complete one another. Gill weighs the comparison man by man; Ellicott weighs it by the depth and reach of the poison. The lesson they draw together is that an emblem set up against the express command becomes, in time and in effect, an idol, however orthodox the intention that raised it. Between them they vindicate the prophet's superlative: Jeroboam's sin was worst not because his private wickedness outran Solomon's, but because he institutionalised apostasy at the fountain-head of the nation's life, changing 'the truth of God into a lie' and fixing a corruption that outlived him for centuries.
10:
The sentence now falls on the house of Jeroboam, and the witnesses attend to the deliberate coarseness of its language. The grammatical-historical commentators do the philology. Ellicott parses the twofold phrase: 'him that is shut up and him that is left' (like the older and younger of Joshua 6:21) is a formula for 'every male,' and the added 'him that pisseth against the wall,' used with 'possibly a touch of contempt,' means that even the lowest shall be destroyed. The closing image, 'as a man taketh away dung,' carries the same scornful tone to its end: the house of Jeroboam is the filth polluting the sacred band of Israel, to be swept away by the besom of destruction (Psalms 83:10). Keil and Delitzsch agree that the second phrase is a set expression for the whole population of males, bond and free alike, so that the sweep of the sentence spares no branch of the family.

The Wesleyan Clarke agrees on the sense, insisting that the blunt Hebrew phrase 'should be thus rendered wherever it occurs,' and that it signifies simply 'every male' — no line of Jeroboam's is to be spared.

The Reformed Gill states the substance plainly: God 'will bring evil upon the house of Jeroboam,' calamities, destruction, and ruin. The witnesses do not divide over meaning; where they add to one another is in the moral colour of the words. Henry catches the retributive fitness the grammar carries: the king who worshipped 'dunghill-deities' has his own family removed 'as a great dunghill,' for 'noble and royal families, if wicked, are no better in God's account.' That is exactly the note Ellicott hears in the grammar, and the totality of the threat — shut up and left, bond and free, high and low — leaves the reader in no doubt that this is no passing chastisement but the uprooting of a dynasty. The judgment answers the crime in kind: a king who set up base gods has his royal line reckoned as refuse, and the throne he schemed to secure by idolatry becomes the very thing that dooms it, a warning written large that the mightiest houses stand no higher in God's account than the meanest.
11:
The threat of an unburied end — the dogs in the city, the fowls in the field — leads the witnesses into the ancient horror of a corpse denied its grave. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the setting. Ellicott identifies the actors exactly: the 'dogs' are 'the half-wild dogs, the scavengers of every Eastern city,' the 'fowls of the air' the vultures and other birds of prey; and he notes the same judgment repeated on other doomed houses (1 Kings 21:24; Jeremiah 36:30). He reaches for the theology beneath the dread: in ancient times the natural horror of insult to the dead was sharpened by the notion that denial of burial somehow touched the departed soul; and whatever lingered of such ideas in Israel, their whole law and ritual gave 'due honour to the body in life, as consecrated to God,' teaching that the body 'was a part of the true man,' and so deepening the repugnance with which reverent feeling regards outrage on the dead. Keil and Delitzsch add that the same fearful sentence is pronounced, almost word for word, on later houses that walked in Jeroboam's way, so that it becomes a formula of covenant judgment upon apostate dynasties.

The Wesleyan Clarke states the plain meaning: to be eaten by dogs and vultures is to have no honourable burial, and 'not to come into the sepulchres of their fathers' — for a royal house, a public disgrace.

The Reformed Gill compresses the whole clause to its point: 'the meaning is, that they should have no burial.' The witnesses do not contend; together they let the sentence make its intended impression. A dynasty that had grasped at permanence is promised the most transient of ends, its bodies left as carrion where they fall. Because Israel honoured the body as God's own, the withholding of burial was felt as the last indignity, a visible token that the house so treated had put itself outside the mercy that even the dead may claim. Thus evil pursues the sinner past his death; and the promise, twice more fulfilled in the annals of the northern kings (1 Kings 16:4), stands here as the seal of Ahijah's word.
12:
The prophet dismisses the queen with a word that is at once a command and a sign, and the witnesses read the poignancy folded into it. The Reformed Gill catches the urgency — 'Arise thou therefore, get thee to thine own house' — bidding her go home 'with all haste, as soon as she could.' The sentence is brief, but it turns the whole errand back on itself: the woman who came the long road to Shiloh to learn her son's fate is sent hurrying home to meet it.

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the timing as a deliberate token. That the child should die at the very moment 'when thy feet enter into the city' was to be a sign to her of the accomplishment of all the rest of the threatenings — much as the death of Eli's two sons in one day was made a sign to him (1 Samuel 2:34). The heavy providence was aimed to instruct both family and kingdom; and Henry feels the sharpness of it for the mother, 'that she should not reach home time enough to see her son alive.' The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the plain reading of the clause, that the boy's death is bound to the very instant of her homecoming, a coincidence too exact to be chance and therefore a sign.

The witnesses do not divide; the verse's brevity is itself part of its meaning, and they draw out what it withholds. There is a strange mercy hidden in the severity, which the next verse will name outright — the child is taken before he can be swept up in his father's ruin. For now the emphasis lies on the certainty of the word: the queen is given no medicine to fetch, no rite to perform, only a road to walk and a death to meet at the end of it. The very exactness of the sign — not a day sooner, not a step later than her entering the city — is meant to authenticate everything else Ahijah has spoken, so that when the small grief is fulfilled to the letter, the larger doom over the house of Jeroboam may be believed without doubt.
13:
Amid a sentence of destruction, one soul is singled out for mourning and a grave, and the witnesses gather with unusual tenderness around the 'some good thing' found in the child. The grammatical-historical Ellicott finds the note singularly pathetic: an early death, in peace and with due mourning, is 'the only reward which can be given to piety in the time of coming judgment,' much like the word spoken to Josiah before the fall of Judah (2 Kings 22:18). He is careful to add that the Old Testament does not, like the Greek story of Cleobis and Bito, treat early death as Heaven's ordinary blessing; life is usually seen as the sphere of God's favour, and only exceptional passages (Job 3:11) speak otherwise — which makes this concession, wrung from judgment, the more moving.

The Puritan Matthew Henry mines the character of the boy. 'Those are good in whom are good things towards the Lord God of Israel,' and where there is even a little of such grace God, who seeks it, sees it and is pleased with it; 'a little grace goes a great way with great people.' To be good in the house of Jeroboam, Henry says, is 'a miracle of divine grace,' like being in the fiery furnace 'unhurt, unsinged,' and pious dispositions in the young have 'a peculiar beauty and lustre.' And the child's removal is mercy: 'the righteous are removed from the evil to come,' and 'God often takes those soonest whom he loves best,' heaven being the fittest place for them.

The Wesleyan Clarke states the governing principle: 'far be it from God to destroy the righteous with the wicked; God respects even a little good, because it is a seed from himself.' The Reformed Gill adds the public dimension — all Israel will mourn and bury him because he was the hopeful heir, on whom their expectations of a better, more religious reign had rested. The witnesses do not divide; they land together on a double truth. The one honourable grave in a doomed dynasty is both a testimony that God marks and honours the faintest true devotion, and a bad omen for the house, since it is 'a bad sign for a family when the best in it are buried out of it,' and what was valuable is picked out before the rest is given to the fire.
14:
The oracle lifts its eyes past the sick child to a coming avenger, and the witnesses divide chiefly over the meaning of a cryptic closing phrase. The grammatical-historical commentators name the man and press the grammar. Ellicott identifies the promised king as Baasha, who like Jeroboam would have his own probation and fail it, drawing down doom on his house (1 Kings 15:27). Keil and Delitzsch expound the abrupt words 'but what? even now' as Ahijah interrupting himself: God 'will raise himself up a king who will cut off the house of Jeroboam this day — but what do I say? even now,' as though the deliverer were already at hand, the broken syntax a deliberate quickening of pace.

The real interest is the translation crux, and Ellicott lays out the whole range of readings. 'The exact meaning of these words has been much disputed': the Septuagint gives 'and what? even now,' the Vulgate 'in this day and in this time,' the Chaldee Targum 'what is now, and what besides shall be.' On the whole he judges the received English version to yield 'a simple and striking sense' — 'in that day; but what say I? the judgment is even now at hand' — and he compares our Lord's very similar self-interruption, 'I am come to send fire on the earth; and what will I, if it be already kindled?' (Luke 12:49), an impatience of judgment that will not wait.

The Reformed Gill contents himself with the plain identification — 'Baasha is meant' — pointing forward to the history that will record the fulfilment. The witnesses do not quarrel over substance; the difference is one of rendering, not of doctrine. Whether the phrase is read as 'even now' or 'in this day and this time,' all agree on its thrust: the judgment is no distant threat but a thing pressing so near that the prophet breaks his own sentence to say so. What reads at first as an obscure aside becomes, in their hands, the most urgent line of the oracle — the executioner is chosen, the door stands open, and within a single short reign Baasha will make the word good.
15:
The judgment now widens from the dynasty to the whole nation, and the witnesses read the image of the wind-shaken reed and the threat of exile as the chapter's furthest reach. The grammatical-historical commentators unfold the figure. Keil and Delitzsch expand the compressed simile: God will so smite Israel that it will sway to and fro 'like a reed in the water moved by a strong wind,' having no firm hold to resist the storm, and will 'thrust them out of the good land' and 'scatter them beyond the river' — the Euphrates — as Moses had threatened the covenant-breaker (Deuteronomy 29:27), so that Ahijah speaks no novelty but applies an old sanction now come due. Ellicott marks this as 'the first prophecy of future captivity,' pronounced against the northern kingdom for its share in Jeroboam's idolatry and in the worse abominations of the 'groves.'

Ellicott then guards the prophecy from fatalism with a note of real theological weight. Every such utterance, he insists, stands under the principle of Jeremiah 18:7 — that if a threatened nation turn from its evil, God will repent of the evil he thought to do; 'the prophecy uttered does not foreclose the probation of future ages,' and here again the foreknowledge of God does not preclude the freedom and responsibility of man. He also corrects the rendering 'groves': the word is properly Asherah, an idol — 'the straight stem of a tree, surmounted by an emblem of the goddess' — which the older versions wrongly turned into 'grove' (Deuteronomy 7:5).

The Reformed Gill draws the nearer, political sense of the reed: it signifies 'the fluctuating and uncertain condition Israel should be in future reigns, through civil wars, and the translation of the kingdom into different families,' so that there was continual disquiet and no settled peace. The Wesleyan Clarke simply points ahead to the partial fulfilment on Jeroboam's own house (1 Kings 15:29). The witnesses do not divide; together they read the one image on two horizons — the near unsteadiness of a throne that will never rest in one line, and the far uprooting that carries the ten tribes across the river — while Ellicott keeps the door of mercy open, that judgment threatened is judgment that repentance might yet have turned aside.
16:
The oracle closes on the refrain that will haunt the rest of the book — Jeroboam 'who did sin, and who made Israel to sin' — and the witnesses read it as the theology of a leader's guilt. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the verse in its narrative frame: God 'shall give Israel up' because of these sins, and the historian immediately carries the word into fulfilment, the child dying, the mother mourning, the reign drawing to its close, as if to show the judgment already in motion. The clause is not an aside but the hinge on which the nation's whole future turns.

The Reformed Gill glosses the surrender simply — God will give Israel up 'into the hands of their enemies' — and lets the double verb stand: Jeroboam both sinned himself and made others sin, and both are gathered into the ground of the nation's overthrow.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, gathering the sense of the whole oracle, draws the moral that the witnesses land on together. If the blind lead the blind, both fall into the ditch; and 'families and kingdoms are ruined by sin, ruined by the wickedness of the heads of them.' The peculiar horror of Jeroboam's case is its multiplying effect: 'if great men do wickedly, they involve many others both in the guilt and in the snare,' and such men 'go to hell with a long train.' Their condemnation, Henry warns, will be the more intolerable, 'for they must answer, not only for their own sins, but for the sins which others have been drawn into and kept in by their influence.' The witnesses do not contend; they hear in the summary sentence both a specific verdict and a standing law. The exile foretold in 1 Kings 14:15 is here given its moral ground — a whole people handed over because one man taught them to worship amiss — and the reader is left to feel the awful arithmetic of leadership, where a single ruler's apostasy is reckoned as the sin of the nation he seduced, so that Jeroboam becomes the standing type of such ruinous leadership.
17:
The scene shifts from Shiloh back to the royal house, and the witnesses attend to the mournful precision with which the prophecy is fulfilled. The grammatical-historical Ellicott fixes the place. From this incidental notice it appears that Jeroboam had moved his residence, whether for a time or permanently, to Tirzah — 'a place renowned for beauty' (Song of Solomon 6:4), lying further from the hostile frontier than Shechem, and continuing as the capital of the northern kingdom until Samaria was founded. Its site, he notes, is generally identified with a spot still called Telluzah, some nine miles north-east of Shechem in the high ground of Mount Ephraim.

The Reformed Gill keeps to the human motion of the verse: Jeroboam's wife 'arose, and departed,' setting out upon the prophet's heavy speech, carrying home the very sentence she had gone so far to escape.

The witnesses do not divide, but the placing of the name Tirzah gives their reading its edge. The queen returns to the fairest city of the realm, a place whose very name breathed delight, and it is there, at the threshold of the door, that the child dies — exactly as Ahijah had foretold, at the moment her feet crossed into the city (1 Kings 14:12). The beauty of the setting throws the grief into sharper relief: no loveliness of place can shut out the death that the word of God has appointed, and the delightful capital becomes the stage of the first sorrow of the doomed house. Matthew Henry, reflecting on the same removal, observes that death, 'which will stain its beauty and embitter all its delights, cannot be shut out' from even so sweet a spot, and pictures the mother left there to attend her son's funeral and await the ruin of her house. The witnesses thus let the geography preach: the queen hurries home to a place of joy only to bury her son there, and the exactness of the timing seals the certainty of every heavier word Ahijah has yet spoken. They note, too, the swiftness of the stroke: the boy who lay sick as she set out is dead before she can cross her own threshold, so that the long road to Shiloh and back frames a single, unalterable decree, and flight from the word only carries the queen to the very place appointed for its fulfilment.
18:
The burial and the mourning are recorded in a single breath, and the witnesses read the verse as the seal set on the prophet's word. The Reformed Gill notes the honour of the interment — 'they buried him' in a manner 'suitable to his rank and pedigree' — the one member of Jeroboam's house to be laid in a grave rather than left to the dogs and the birds.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch group the verse with the close of the reign and stress the clause that governs it: all Israel mourned for him and buried him 'according to the word of the Lord, which he spake by the hand of his servant Ahijah the prophet.' The emphasis falls not on the funeral but on the fulfilment; the double naming of the authority — the word of the Lord spoken by the hand of his servant Ahijah — turns a family burial into a public vindication of prophecy, and the historian is careful to say that the event answered the prophecy to the letter.

The witnesses do not divide, and the brevity of the verse concentrates its force. Everything Ahijah foretold of the child now comes true at once. The boy alone of the house is mourned and buried, because in him alone was 'found some good thing towards the Lord God of Israel' (1 Kings 14:13); the whole nation laments him, as the prophet said they would, for he was the hope of a better reign; and he dies first of all the family, taken from the evil to come while his kindred are reserved for judgment. The mourning of 'all Israel' also quietly measures the loss, for the people grieve not merely a prince but the one heart in the palace that inclined to the true God. Above all, the twice-named authority — the word of the Lord spoken by his servant Ahijah — makes this small and tender detail a proof of the whole. If it has come to pass exactly as spoken, the larger sentence over the dynasty and the nation cannot fail; the reader is meant to carry that confidence forward into all that follows, and to reckon the word of God, however heavy, as a word that does not return void.
19:
The chronicler turns from the death to the record, and the witnesses read the summary formula as a lesson in what Scripture chooses to remember. The grammatical-historical Ellicott makes the selectivity his theme. The preceding verse closes the detailed account of Jeroboam's reign; 'his exaltation and the promise to him, his idolatry and its punishment, are all that the historian cares to narrate.' Everything else is folded into two phrases — 'how he warred' (1 Kings 14:30) and 'how he reigned.' It is likely enough, Ellicott grants, that the reign was prosperous in peace and war, though the attempt to subdue Judah failed (2 Chronicles 13:2); yet all of it the sacred record passes over, and commemorates him only as 'Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin.' The sacred historian, he presses, is no mere annalist: he weighs a reign not by its victories or its length but by its fidelity to God, and by that scale a possibly prosperous rule shrinks to a single clause of shame.

The Reformed Gill fills the bare word 'warred' with its content: Jeroboam warred with Rehoboam, and with Abijam his son, who proved more than a match for him. The Wesleyan Clarke simply points the reader to the fuller narrative in the Chronicles for the 'important particulars' the book of Kings omits (2 Chronicles 13:1), tacitly acknowledging that ampler records once existed and were passed over.

The witnesses do not divide; together they draw out the theology hidden in an archival note. The 'book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel' held, no doubt, the annals of battles and buildings that any court would prize, but the inspired historian is governed by a different measure of greatness. What the world would record he leaves to lost registers; what he preserves is the moral verdict. The very brevity is a sermon: a man may fill the annals of his age and yet be summed up before God in one damning clause. Gill's naming of the wars and Clarke's pointer to the Chronicles serve the same irony the formula carries: the fullest human records of a reign may preserve precisely what God counts least, while the single thing the sacred writer keeps is the verdict the king would least wish remembered.
20:
The reign closes with a bare notice of its length and the king's death, and the witnesses fill the terse formula with the weight of a life summed up. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record the plain facts — 'the days which Jeroboam reigned were two and twenty years: and he slept with his fathers, and Nadab his son reigned in his stead' — and pass at once to the successor through whom the promised destruction of the house begins to fall.

The Reformed Gill supplies the chronology and, from the parallel record, the manner of the end. Jeroboam 'outlived Rehoboam five years, and lived to the second year of the reign of' Asa; but the softness of the phrase 'slept with his fathers' should not mislead, for the Chronicler tells us the Lord 'struck him' with a sore disease, 'so that he died' miserably (2 Chronicles 13:20). Gill notes the swift unravelling that followed: the crown passed to a son who lost it, and his life too, and all the lives of his family, within two years.

The witnesses do not divide; they read the quiet obituary against the loud oracle that preceded it. The formula 'he slept with his fathers' is the common courtesy of the annals, but here it covers a death under divine judgment, and the reader who remembers the prophet's word sees the sentence beginning to fall. The dynasty Jeroboam schemed to secure by a rival religion is already crumbling: he dies smitten, his son Nadab succeeds only to be cut off with the whole house by Baasha, exactly the avenger Ahijah had promised (1 Kings 14:14). Even the neat chronological equation — twenty-two years, an overlap with Rehoboam and then with Asa — serves the larger theme, fixing the apostate's reign in the calendar of the kings only to show how short-lived was the greatness he had grasped at, and how surely the word of the Lord overtook it. The brevity of the epitaph is itself eloquent, for a reign of two-and-twenty years, longer than many, is dismissed in a single verse, its length no measure of its worth in the sight of God; and the crown passes at once to Nadab, in whose brief rule the whole house will be swept away.
21:
The narrative crosses the border into Judah, and the witnesses read the opening notice of Rehoboam's reign as a study in privilege wasted. The Puritan Matthew Henry frames the tragedy. It is telling, he says, that 'no good is said of the king': Rehoboam was 'forty-one years old when he began to reign,' born in the last year of David and formed in the best days of Solomon, 'yet he lived not up to these advantages.' The lesson Henry draws is sombre — 'Solomon's defection at last did more to corrupt him than his wisdom and devotion had done to give him good principles.' He reigned in Jerusalem, 'the city where God put his name,' with every opportunity to know his duty, and he had it not in his heart to do it.

Henry then presses the twice-repeated note that his mother 'was Naamah, an Ammonitess.' It was strange, he reflects, that David married Solomon to a heathen woman, and stranger still that the marriage bore such bitter fruit; and he warns that 'none can imagine how lasting and how fatal the consequences may be of being unequally yoked with unbelievers.' Rehoboam's continual war with Jeroboam, and his comparatively short reign of seventeen years, complete the mournful account.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds a small observation from the names — the coincidence that Jeroboam's son and Rehoboam were each connected with the name Abijah, perhaps because the births fell about the same time — and the Reformed Gill marks the bare political fact that Rehoboam 'reigned in Judah,' over the two tribes, while Jeroboam held the other ten. The witnesses do not divide; the force of their reading lies in the contrast the verse quietly sets up. Rehoboam had what Jeroboam lacked — the temple, the city of God's choice, the line of David, an upbringing under the wisest of kings — and squandered it, while the heathen blood of his mother and the idolatry of his father's old age worked their slow poison. The repeated 'Ammonitess' is no idle genealogy but a pointing finger, tracing the apostasy of Judah, which the following verses record, back to a marriage that yoked the house of David to a stranger's gods.
22:
The scene widens to the whole of Judah, and the witnesses read the charge that the people 'did evil' as the harvest of seed sown in Solomon's reign. The grammatical-historical commentators draw on the fuller record in the Chronicles. Keil and Delitzsch note that Rehoboam began better than he ended: warned by the prophet that the loss of the ten tribes was God's punishment for Solomon's idolatry, he 'followed the law of God with earnestness' in his first years, fortifying cities and ordering his house (2 Chronicles 11:5). Ellicott sharpens the point: for three years 'they walked in the way of David and Solomon' (2 Chronicles 11:17), but with returning security the wholesome reaction passed and gave way to 'an extraordinarily reckless plunge into abominations of the worst kind.' He notes the striking grammar — the evil is ascribed not to the king, as with most reigns, but to 'the people at large,' for Rehoboam himself seems to have been weak, 'unfit for taking the initiative either in good or evil'; the apostasy was 'the harvest of the deadly seed sown by the commanding influence of Solomon,' under whose idolatry the young men had grown up.

The Reformed Gill fixes the timing — the evil came 'at the end of three years, from the beginning of the reign of Rehoboam' — marking the exact point where reformation curdled into revolt.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gives the verse its sting. Judah, 'the only professing people God had in the world,' provoked him 'to jealousy, as the adulterous wife provokes her husband by breaking the marriage-covenant,' and did 'abominable things, above all that their fathers had done.' Neither the magnificence of the temple nor the pomp of the priesthood could hold them; 'nothing less than the pouring out of the Spirit from on high' could have kept them true. The witnesses do not divide; whether they weigh the guilt as worse than the fathers' because of the greater light of a more advanced age, as Ellicott suggests, or as spiritual adultery against a covenant God, as Henry does, they agree that Judah's fall was no sudden lapse but the ripening of an inheritance of compromise, the more culpable for the greater light against which it was committed.
23:
The catalogue of Judah's idolatry — high places, images, and groves on every hill and under every green tree — draws the witnesses into the history of forbidden worship. The grammatical-historical commentators do the archaeology. Keil and Delitzsch explain that the 'images' are mazzeboth, not statues of gods but memorial stones such as Jacob set up (Genesis 28:18), originally dedicated to Jehovah yet forbidden under the law, chiefly because the Canaanites had raised such pillars to Baal beside his altars (Exodus 34:13), so that the worship of the true God was unconsciously identified with the worship of Baal. Ellicott agrees that the 'images' were stone pillars and the 'groves' the asherahs — 'wooden stumps of trees, possibly surmounted by some rude representation of the deity' — and traces the same double history: both might be 'either unauthorised emblems of God's presence or images of false gods,' but the strength of the language here, and the notice of the next verse, show that 'the grosser idolatry is referred to.'

The Reformed Gill presses the inexcusability of it. The high places, he allows, had been 'connived at' before the temple was built, while the tabernacle was unfixed; but afterwards they were unlawful, 'and the tribe of Judah could have no excuse for them, who had the temple in their tribe.' The one people with the true sanctuary in their midst went whoring after emblems on every hill.

The witnesses do not divide; together they trace the fatal logic by which emblem becomes idol. What began, perhaps, as an aid to devotion — a stone to mark a divine meeting, a pillar to fix the memory of grace — slid, under the pull of the surrounding nations, into the very thing the law had forbidden. Ellicott's remark that such things 'eventually tend to become' objects of worship states the principle the whole verse illustrates: that the setting of images beside the altar of the Lord, however innocently intended, opens the door through which 'the transition to worship of many gods is unhappily only too easy,' and that Judah, with the temple at hand, had least excuse of all for walking through it.
24:
The list of abominations reaches its lowest point with the notice of 'sodomites in the land,' and the witnesses handle the grim detail with sober philology. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the word. Keil and Delitzsch identify the Hebrew qadesh as a generic term for the male and female temple-prostitutes, the men self-mutilated 'in religious frenzy in honour of the Canaanitish goddess of nature' — the Galli of the Romans — who had made their way into Judah as idolatry gained the upper hand (Deuteronomy 23:18), appearing there as strangers, the debris of a heathen worship. Ellicott dwells on 'the horrible significance in the derivation of this word, which is properly consecrated, or devoted,' for it marks 'the license, and even the sanction, of unnatural lusts in those consecrated to the abominations of Nature-worship' (2 Kings 23:7). He reads their appearance as 'the climax of the infinite corruption which had set in,' rivalling and even exceeding the abominations of the land's old inhabitants.

The Wesleyan Clarke agrees on the sense of the term — the kedeshim were 'consecrated persons; persons who had devoted themselves, in practices of the greatest impurity, to the service of the most impure idols.' The Reformed Gill notes that 'filthy actions of this nature usually attended idolatrous practices among the heathens, in their temples and groves.'

The witnesses do not divide; their common reading exposes the deepest evil in the chapter — not merely vice, but vice sanctified. What the surrounding nations practised as religion Judah now imported under the cover of ritual, and Ellicott's phrase names the horror precisely: 'to find them sanctioned under cover of religious ritual marks a lower depth still.' The commentators are at one that such corruption is no mark of a primitive age but can flourish amid knowledge and civilisation; and the verse's closing clause — 'according to all the abominations of the nations which the Lord cast out before the children of Israel' — turns the indictment into a warning. Judah is now doing the very things for which the Canaanites were dispossessed, and stands, on the same principle, under the same threat of being cast out in its turn.
25:
The invasion of Shishak breaks upon Judah in Rehoboam's fifth year, and the witnesses read it as judgment tempered with mercy, and as history remarkably confirmed. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the fuller record and the external evidence. Keil and Delitzsch describe the campaign: Shishak 'came up against Jerusalem' with a powerful army, took the fortified cities, and would have made an end of the kingdom 'if God had not had compassion,' sparing it after the king and princes humbled themselves at the word of the prophet Shemaiah (2 Chronicles 12:2). They identify Shishak with Sheshonk, first king of the twenty-second dynasty, whose expedition against Judah is carved in the great bas-relief at Karnak, where more than a hundred and thirty captive figures are led in cords by the god Ammon. Ellicott confirms the point: the inscription enumerating the conquests of Sheshenk names cities in both Judah and Israel, and 'this record is remarkably confirmed' by the monument.

Ellicott adds a political reading. The Israelite cities among the conquered suggest that Shishak came 'as allies of Jeroboam,' taking, or helping him to take, towns hostile to him, much as an earlier Pharaoh had taken Gezer and given it to Israel (1 Kings 9:16); he judges it 'not unlikely that the whole invasion was instigated by Jeroboam,' out of the same desire to crush Judah that later drove the war with Abijam.

The Reformed Gill fixes the chastening timing — the blow fell 'two years after he and his people fell into the above wicked practices' — so that the reader sees cause and effect. The witnesses do not divide; together they set the invasion in a triple frame. It is punishment, following hard upon the apostasy just described; it is mercy, since the humbling of king and people turned aside the destruction the army might have wrought; and it is history, its outline written not only in the Chronicles but on the temple wall of Karnak, a heathen king's boast turned, in God's hand, into a witness to the truth of the sacred record. The witnesses feel the mercy most keenly, for the Chronicler tells how the humbled king and princes owned that the Lord is righteous, and the destroyer was stayed at the gate of a city that lay wholly in his power.
26:
Shishak strips the temple and palace of their treasures, and the witnesses read the loss with a mixture of pathos and grim justice. The grammatical-historical commentators note the completeness of the spoil. Keil and Delitzsch record that he 'took away all the shields of gold which Solomon had made,' shields once kept in the house of the forest of Lebanon (1 Kings 10:17) and now carried off with the rest. Ellicott finds 'a touch of pathos in the description of the utter spoil of the treasures in which Solomon and Israel had gloried, and which now served only to buy off the victorious Egyptians.' He draws a careful historical inference from the manner of it: there is 'no notice of any sack of Jerusalem, nor of any desecration of the Temple,' the record implying a surrender of the city and its treasures rather than a storming; and he uses this to refute the theory that Shishak's invasion, like the Gaulish capture of Rome, destroyed Judah's ancient monuments and archives — a theory with 'no historical support from this passage.'

The Wesleyan Clarke measures the magnitude of the plunder. The golden shields 'were three hundred in number, and were all made of beaten gold,' part of 'a booty the most immense ever acquired in one place.' The Reformed Gill adds the political undercurrent, suspecting that Jeroboam, an old friend of Shishak from his days of Egyptian exile, had told him of the treasures and helped draw him against a Rehoboam who was no kin to Pharaoh's house.

The witnesses do not divide; their combined reading turns an inventory of loss into a parable. The gold that David had dedicated and Solomon had multiplied, the visible glory of the kingdom, is handed over in a day to purchase peace from an invader. What idolatry had corrupted within, invasion now carried away without; and the very riches in which the nation had trusted became the ransom of its safety. The pathos Ellicott feels is the moral of the scene: earthly splendour amassed apart from faithfulness proves no defence, and the treasure-house that had been the pride of Jerusalem is emptied to the last shield.
27:
Rehoboam replaces the plundered gold with brass, and the witnesses find in the substitution a small parable of a diminished kingdom. The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads it as more than a curious detail. The notice of the brazen shields, he suggests, is 'perhaps intended as a symbolic representation of the change which had passed upon Judah, by which only the semblance of its old glory remained, and its fine gold had become brass' (Lamentations 4:1). The counterfeit shields, borne in the same state as the originals, keep up appearances over a real impoverishment.

The Reformed Gill states the plain reason for the change: 'the king of Egypt had so stripped him of his gold, that he was not able to replace golden ones.' Necessity, not modesty, dictated the brass; the treasury that Shishak emptied could not be refilled.

The witnesses do not divide, and the meagreness of the material only concentrates their point. There is something almost poignant in the king's care to maintain the outward pomp — shields still carried before him when he went up to the house of the Lord, still glinting in procession — when the substance behind the show was gone. Ellicott's image of 'fine gold become brass' catches exactly the condition of Judah after the apostasy and the invasion: the forms of Solomon's glory preserved, the reality hollowed out. What was true of the shields was true of the nation. A people that had exchanged the worship of the living God for emblems on every high hill now found its splendour likewise exchanged, gold for base metal; and the brazen shields, faithfully polished and paraded, stood as a standing emblem of a greatness that could be imitated but not restored. The verse thus quietly moralises the whole reign: the semblance of the golden age remained, but its heart had been carried off to Egypt. Gill's blunt cause and Ellicott's symbol meet in a single lesson: the shields that Solomon had made of beaten gold are answered now by brass, and the outward order of worship continues while its treasure is gone, so that Judah's punishment is written not only in what the invader took but in what the king could merely counterfeit. The witnesses read the substitution as the epitaph of a squandered inheritance, a kingdom keeping the shape of a glory it could no longer afford.
28:
The brazen shields are borne in state whenever the king goes up to the house of the Lord, and the witnesses read the ceremony as a token of a partial and chastened return. The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the ritual and its implication. Keil and Delitzsch note that the runners carried the shields before the king on his visits to the temple, 'from which we may see that the king was accustomed to go to the temple with solemn pomp,' the new shields kept now in the guard-chamber rather than the treasure-house (Ezekiel 40:7). Ellicott draws the inference the verse invites: 'hence we see that Rehoboam still worshipped in the house of the Lord,' and that after the invasion, as the Chronicler records, 'he humbled himself, and returned to the Lord' (2 Chronicles 12:6). Clarke adds the neat detail that the guard 'probably were just three hundred, answering to the number of the shields.'

The Reformed Gill reads the same movement pastorally. Though Rehoboam had fallen into idolatry, 'he had not wholly forsaken the worship of God in the temple,' and Gill suggests that 'by the late humbling providence he might be stirred up to attend there more frequently.' The chastening of Shishak's invasion drove the king back to the sanctuary he had neglected.

The witnesses do not divide; together they let a ceremonial note carry a spiritual weight. The pomp is real but reduced — brass where gold had been — and yet the king still goes up to the house of the Lord, and that persistence is read as the fruit of humbling. The affliction that stripped the temple became, under God, the means of recalling the king to it. Ellicott and Gill agree that the invasion produced a genuine, if imperfect, repentance; the same providence that carried off the golden shields brought Rehoboam back through the temple doors with the brazen ones. The witnesses thus find in the procession a mingled picture — diminished glory and revived devotion — fitting for a reign that never rose to David's height but did not, in the end, wholly abandon David's God.
29:
The reign of Rehoboam is closed with the customary reference to the royal annals, and the witnesses read the formula as a pointer to the fuller records behind the sacred narrative. The grammatical-historical commentators supply what the reference conceals. Keil and Delitzsch observe that 'further particulars are given in the Chronicles concerning the rest of the acts of Rehoboam' (2 Chronicles 12:15), and they take up the puzzling notice, reserved for the next verse, that there was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam 'the whole time': since nothing is said of open battle, and since the prophet Shemaiah had forbidden Rehoboam's intended attack on the seceding tribes (1 Kings 12:24), the word can only denote 'the hostile feelings and attitude of the two rulers towards one another.' Ellicott adds the interesting cross-reference that the Chronicler names the very authors of the source — the acts of Rehoboam were 'written in the book of Shemaiah the prophet, and of Iddo the seer concerning genealogies' — which shows that even the annals of the kings were in part the work of prophets, so that the registration and the interpretation of a reign lay close together.

The Reformed Gill keeps to the surface of the verse, marking that it gathers up 'the rest of the acts of Rehoboam, and all that he did, in the course of his reign, that was memorable.'

The witnesses do not divide; their joint reading opens a small window on how the book of Kings was made. Behind the spare inspired account stood a body of court records and prophetic writings, to which the historian could refer his readers for the particulars he chose to omit. The formula is thus no idle refrain but an acknowledgement that the history has been sifted from fuller sources with a purpose — the same purpose that governs the whole narrative, to weigh each king by his faithfulness rather than to chronicle his every deed. The naming of Shemaiah and Iddo drives the point home, for the men who wrote the annals were themselves prophets, and even the record of a reign was, in their hands, a moral reckoning rather than a bare register of events.
30:
The single notice that 'there was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all their days' draws the witnesses into a small question of interpretation: what kind of war is meant? The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the possibilities against the silence of the record. Keil and Delitzsch, as at the previous verse, judge that since there was no open, pitched conflict, and since Shemaiah had forbidden Rehoboam's planned invasion of the northern tribes (1 Kings 12:24), the term denotes not campaigns but the settled hostility of the two courts. Ellicott is more cautious still: 'of such war we have no record,' he notes, nor any mention of Israel aiding the Egyptian attack, likely though such aid may have been; the meaning 'may simply be that there was continued enmity, breaking off all peaceful relations,' though in the scantiness of the record 'we can have no certainty that actual war did not take place.'

The Reformed Gill offers the mediating reading. Though Rehoboam, dissuaded by Shemaiah, 'did not enter into an offensive war,' he 'might maintain a defensive war'; and though there were no set battles such as fell out in his son's time, 'there might be skirmishes and bickerings on the borders of their countries.' The enmity was real and armed, even if it never flared into a decisive engagement.

The witnesses do not sharply divide; theirs is a difference of degree rather than of principle. All agree that the two kingdoms lived in unbroken antagonism, the breach opened at the division never healed; the only question is whether the standing hostility broke out into border raids or remained a cold estrangement. Behind the dispute lies a settled fact the commentators feel keenly: the schism that Ahijah's earlier prophecy had sanctioned as God's judgment on Solomon's house hardened, within a single generation, into permanent division, so that brother-nations who should have been one spent their days in mutual enmity. The verse, brief as it is, seals the tragedy of the divided monarchy that the whole chapter has been tracing. The witnesses let the last word of Rehoboam's reign be this unbroken hostility, a standing sign that the kingdom sundered for Solomon's sin would not be knit up again, but would spend its strength, generation after generation, in the quarrel of brothers.
31:
The chapter closes with Rehoboam's death and burial, the second mention of his Ammonite mother, and the accession of his son, and the witnesses read the notice as a meditation on the inheritance of apostasy. The Reformed Gill marks the point of the repeated name: 'his mother's name was Naamah, an Ammonitess, which is repeated, that it might be observed as what was the leading step to his idolatry, and the means of his continuing in it.' The heathen mother is named twice because she is, in Gill's reading, the root of the whole sad reign.

The Wesleyan Clarke presses the theme of heredity hardest. Rehoboam 'was born of a heathen mother, and begotten of an apostate father. From such an impure fountain could sweet water possibly spring?' Righteousness, he observes, 'cannot be propagated, because it is supernatural,' but unrighteousness may, 'for that is a genuine offspring of nature'; and so 'Abijam was the wicked son of an apostate father and heathenish mother.' Yet Clarke guards the point against fatalism with a maxim: 'grace may be grafted on a crab stock; but let none do evil that good may come of it.' He adds his usual textual care over the son's name, which appears in various forms and is given by the Evangelist as Abia (Matthew 1:7), and he notes that the humbling of Shishak's invasion seems to have had a good effect, prompting the king to frequent the temple he had before neglected.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat the double naming more soberly, explaining that the repetition of the queen-mother's name here, as at the reign's opening, may simply reflect the sources the writer used, and they observe that 'Abijam' is the older form of a name later reduced to 'Abijah.' The witnesses do not sharply divide; between Gill's and Clarke's moral reading and the grammarians' textual one, they let the verse round off the chapter's great theme. Two kings have died under the shadow of their sins, and each hands his crown to a son who will walk in his ways. The twice-named Ammonitess stands at the head of Judah's decline as Jeroboam's calves stand at the head of Israel's; and the quiet succession of Abijam to a throne so compromised warns that the consequences of a father's unfaithfulness are seldom buried with him.
